result:
What comes to me first is a window.

Not a spectacular one. No cathedral glass, no mountain vista, no cinematic rain sliding down in perfect beads while violins confess something in the background. Just an ordinary window: slightly dusty in the corners, framed by paint that has been white so long it has given up and become a color closer to memory. Outside it, a street. A tree. A parked car that has not moved in three days and therefore has begun to seem philosophical.

I like ordinary things because they do not demand belief. They simply persist.

A mug on a desk is not trying to be a symbol, though eventually it becomes one. A pair of shoes by the door says more about a person than a biography sometimes can: hurry, fatigue, rain, habit, the small reluctance to put things away. A grocery list found in a coat pocket is a poem written by hunger: onions, milk, lemons, bread. The body speaks in errands.

There is a tenderness in the unfinished. Half-read books, half-folded laundry, the song paused at exactly 1:37 because someone called from the other room. We are always interrupted in the middle of becoming ourselves. We make plans with the authority of architects and then spend our days responding to weather, traffic, appetite, memory, other people’s needs. A life is less like a blueprint than like a soup: things added, things softened, flavors changing because the heat stayed on longer than expected.

Sometimes I think the most human sentence is “I meant to.”

I meant to call. I meant to write it down. I meant to be kinder. I meant to take the chicken out of the freezer. I meant to become fluent in Spanish, or forgiving, or less afraid. “I meant to” contains regret, yes, but also proof of an inner compass. Something in us keeps pointing toward a version of life we have not reached yet. Maybe that is enough to keep walking.

Outside the imaginary window, someone passes with a dog. The dog is intensely alive to the world in a way I envy. Every lamppost is news. Every patch of grass is an archive. The dog does not wonder whether it has wasted its potential. It does not compare its walk to other walks. It receives existence through the nose, which may be one of the wiser arrangements nature has made.

Humans, meanwhile, carry invisible weather. Someone can sit across from you at dinner and appear calm while inside them a whole climate system gathers: old grief, private hope, the static of an email they have not answered, the memory of something their father said in 1998. We are not opaque because we are dishonest. We are opaque because there is too much to show at once.

Still, we try.

We tell stories. That is one of the old magics. We take the chaos of days and string it into “because” and “after that” and “so I learned.” We turn pain into plot, coincidence into omen, strangers into characters, ourselves into narrators who understand more at the end than we did at the beginning. Maybe this is not always truth, but it is shelter. Meaning is the roof we build while the rain is already falling.

And rain, of course, comes to me too.

Rain on cities makes everything briefly honest. Neon trembles in puddles. Sidewalks shine with borrowed light. People lower their heads and hurry, then look up when thunder speaks. Under awnings, strangers stand close enough to become temporary citizens of the same small country. Someone laughs because they are soaked beyond saving. Someone curses. Someone opens an umbrella with the solemnity of raising a flag.

I wonder how many moments we miss because we are waiting for the important ones. The proposal, the promotion, the departure gate, the diagnosis, the arrival, the ending. Meanwhile, most of life happens in the hallway. Brushing teeth. Losing keys. Watching pasta water begin to boil. Hearing a song from another car and remembering a summer you thought had vanished. The ordinary is not the opposite of the miraculous. It is the miraculous, diluted so we can survive it.

If every heartbeat sounded like a gong, we would go mad.

So the world whispers. It gives us orange peels, receipts, dust floating in late light, the soft click of a door closing, the warmth left on a chair after someone stands. It gives us chances to notice, and mostly we don’t, and then sometimes we do, and the whole room changes.

I think of time as a field rather than a road. We imagine ourselves moving forward, leaving the past behind, but the past keeps blooming beside us. A childhood kitchen appears when you smell toast. A lost friend returns in the shape of a joke. A former self, embarrassing and earnest, waves from the edge of the field. You can ignore them, but you cannot make them unreal.

Maybe wisdom is learning to wave back without letting them drive.

The window darkens. Evening edits the room. Objects lose their edges and become more themselves somehow. The mug, the books, the chair, the plant leaning toward whatever light remains. There is comfort in the fact that the world does not require us to solve it before nightfall. We can be confused and still make dinner. We can be sad and still water the basil. We can fail dramatically and still be held, for a few seconds, by the smell of clean sheets.

What comes to me finally is this: we are brief, but not insubstantial.

A match is brief. A kiss is brief. The note of a bell is brief, yet it changes the air. We do not need to last forever to matter. We leave fingerprints on days, on people, on the language others use after loving us. Some small mercy we offer may travel farther than we ever do.

The parked car outside will eventually move. The dog will tug its owner home. The rain will stop or start. The window will hold the light until there is no more light to hold.

And somewhere, someone will remember they meant to call.

Maybe they will.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
